Management used a portion of this strength to lift the quarterly dividend and repurchase $32 million of stock, though buybacks remain modest relative to the available excess.

Asset Quality Appears Stable

Credit performance remained strong, with net charge‑offs running at just 10 basis points for the quarter, a level consistent with benign credit conditions. The allowance for loan losses covered 130 basis points of total loans, and management described the overall credit book as “pristine,” reinforcing confidence in the portfolio.

Active Loan Repricing and Attractive New Loan Yields

Management repriced about $400 million of loans in the quarter and expects another $1.8 billion to reprice later in the year at roughly a 5.8% yield. New loan opportunities are coming at spreads of about 300 basis points over similar‑maturity Treasuries, positioning the bank to support net interest income as older, lower‑yielding assets roll off.

Product and Fee Momentum

Payments revenue followed its usual seasonal pattern but still posted year‑over‑year growth, signalling that underlying activity continues to build. Commercial payments programs are fueling this expansion, and management expressed confidence that fee and payments momentum will ramp further as the year progresses.

Industry Recognition and Workforce

Central Bancompany earned recognition as one of America’s Best Banks by Forbes and was ranked the best‑performing U.S. public bank over $10 billion in assets by S&P Global Market Intelligence. Executives credited the nearly 3,000 full‑time employees whose service and client focus underpin the bank’s operating performance and brand.

Effective Deployment of Excess Liquidity

To combat drag from excess cash, the bank stepped up securities purchases in March and April, redeploying liquidity into investments yielding about 4.30% in April. This shift, focused on improving asset yields while maintaining prudent duration, is intended to gradually lift earnings without sacrificing balance sheet resilience.

Margin Pressure from Excess Liquidity and Seasonality

Excess liquidity weighed on net interest margin in the first quarter, while lower prepayment fees contributed to a 3 basis‑point decline in loan yields. Higher average public funds produced a seasonal shift in the deposit mix that pushed funding costs higher, though management expects these seasonal effects to ease in coming quarters.

Concentrated Increase in Commercial Delinquencies

A modest uptick in delinquencies was tied to commercial credits concentrated in a handful of markets and specific clients, making the issue more idiosyncratic than systemic. Management expects these problem credits to be resolved, viewing them as a near‑term blemish rather than a sign of broad credit deterioration.

Incremental Public Company Costs and Conversion Expense

The company highlighted roughly $5 million of additional annual expenses tied to being a public company, noting that much of this run rate is already reflected in first‑quarter results. A core system conversion, with only about $700,000 capitalized in the quarter, is also elevating noninterest expense near term but is expected to support longer‑term efficiency.

No Immediate M&A Despite Large Excess Capital

Despite holding excess capital equal to more than half of tangible book value and engaging in active strategic discussions, management emphasized that no acquisition is imminent. With only $32 million of shares repurchased so far, the bank still has considerable dry powder to pursue deals or accelerate buybacks when valuations and opportunities align.

Deposit Dynamics and Competitive Environment

Executives described a highly competitive deposit landscape in which repricing will be gradual, given that 90% of deposits are non‑maturity balances. They expect deposit costs, which show a low‑20s beta to rate changes, to decline over several quarters, suggesting that margin improvement will be steady but not abrupt.

Forward‑Looking Guidance

Looking ahead, management expects net interest margin and earnings to benefit as roughly $1.8 billion of loans reprice this year at around 5.8% and as excess cash is reinvested at about 4.30% with an average duration near four years. Deposit costs already fell about 5 basis points quarter over quarter, excluding seasonal public funds expected to decline in the second and third quarters, while ample excess capital, solid asset quality, and manageable public company expenses should support continued net interest income growth off a 4.36% first‑quarter margin.

Central Bancompany’s earnings call painted the picture of a bank balancing strong current performance with deliberate repositioning for a shifting rate and funding environment. With robust profitability, excellent credit quality, and substantial excess capital, the company appears well placed to weather near‑term margin pressure and emerge with enhanced earnings power, keeping it firmly on investors’ watchlists.

The Specialty Equipment Rentals segment remained the profit engine, with third‑party revenue climbing 16% year over year to $194 million. Segment adjusted EBITDA rose 23% to $105 million, pushing margin to an impressive 51.5% and demonstrating the high‑return, recurring nature of the rental model.

STEM Segment Growth and Margin Expansion

In the Sales, Truck and Equipment, and Manufacturing (STEM) segment, third‑party revenue increased 5% year over year to $268 million. Parts and service revenue grew about 17%, helping segment adjusted EBITDA reach $33 million, with margin at 9% thanks to cost‑out initiatives and productivity improvements.

Expanding Backlog Supports Future Sales

Order trends remained favorable, with new sales backlog ending the quarter at $411 million, up $76 million sequentially. Management noted that backlog continued to build into the second quarter and now stands above $425 million, providing visibility into future equipment sales.

Guidance Affirmed and EBITDA Outlook Raised

The company reaffirmed its 2026 consolidated revenue outlook of $2.005 billion to $2.12 billion, implying 3% to 9% top‑line growth. It also raised adjusted EBITDA guidance to a range of $415 million to $440 million, targeting 8% to 15% year‑over‑year EBITDA growth as rental performance and margin initiatives gain traction.

Capital Allocation Shifts Toward Cash Generation

Custom Truck plans to scale back net rental fleet investment to roughly $150 million to $170 million in 2026, down from $250 million in 2025. With tighter capital spending, the company expects to generate more than $50 million of levered free cash flow this year and to push net leverage below 4x by year‑end, on the way to about 3x in 2027.

Balance Sheet Strength and Liquidity Headroom

Net debt stood at $1.65 billion against trailing 12‑month adjusted EBITDA above $408 million, leaving net leverage just over 4x but improving about 30 basis points sequentially. Liquidity appears solid, with $257 million of asset‑based revolver availability and potential to add roughly $190 million of extra capacity through an upsizing.

The company highlighted that its rental fleet has an average age of under three years, among the youngest in the industry. Management believes this modern fleet not only supports better service and reliability but also positions Custom Truck favorably ahead of stricter Environmental Protection Agency emissions standards coming in 2027.

High Leverage Still a Key Risk

Despite the improving metrics, management acknowledged that leverage remains elevated in the near term. The strategy now hinges on meeting free cash flow targets and executing on working capital reductions, which are critical to bringing net leverage meaningfully lower over the next two years.

Inventory levels remain high at roughly 7 to 7.5 months on hand after a typical seasonal build in the first quarter. The company aims to cut that ratio to below six months by year‑end, with working capital release expected to be a major driver of free cash flow and balance‑sheet repair.

Arcbest’s latest earnings call struck a cautiously optimistic tone as management balanced solid revenue growth and record productivity gains against clear pressure on profitability. Executives highlighted strong operational execution in the Asset‑Light segment and meaningful cost savings, while acknowledging margin headwinds in the Asset‑Based business and the impact of fuel volatility and higher operating expenses.

Consolidated Revenue Growth Signals Resilience

Arcbest posted first‑quarter revenue of $1.0 billion, up 3% year over year, demonstrating that the top line is holding up despite a choppy freight environment. Management framed this as evidence of the company’s ability to retain and win business across its portfolio even as the broader trucking and logistics market remains competitive and price sensitive.

Asset‑Light Segment Delivers Momentum and Record Productivity

Asset‑Light revenue rose to $378 million, up 7% on a daily basis, with shipments per day up 10% to a first‑quarter record. Employee productivity surged 26% to record levels and SG&A expense per shipment fell 15% to a new low, supporting $3 million of non‑GAAP operating income and a $4 million improvement versus last year.

Asset‑Based Volumes Improve Alongside Pricing Discipline

In the Asset‑Based unit, daily shipments increased 2% year over year to nearly 20,000, while daily tonnage climbed 7% thanks to a 5% rise in weight per shipment. The company implemented deferred price increases averaging 6% in the quarter, its strongest pricing action since 2022, underscoring a focus on revenue quality even as freight mix shifts toward heavier loads.

Operational Cost Savings and AI‑Driven Efficiency Gains

Arcbest reported that continuous improvement training across roughly three‑quarters of its network has already generated $32 million in annualized cost savings. An AI‑enabled city route optimization program added another $15 million in annualized savings, with further phases planned, positioning the company to structurally reduce costs as volumes recover.

Product and Technology Investments Enhance Customer Offering

Management spotlighted the launch of ArcBestView in May, a platform that allows customers to quote, book, and track across the company’s logistics solutions in one place. Expanded digital capabilities and a broader dynamic quote pool are improving shipment visibility and selection, contributing to heavier, higher‑quality dynamic shipments and supporting yield.

Constructive Near‑Term Operating Outlook for Asset‑Based

Executives signaled a positive near‑term trend, expecting the Asset‑Based operating ratio to improve about 400 to 500 basis points sequentially in the second quarter, versus a typical seasonal improvement of roughly 350 basis points. They cited a stronger commercial pipeline, continued pricing discipline, and momentum in revenue and tonnage as key drivers of the anticipated improvement.

April Shows Accelerating Asset‑Light Commercial Momentum

April data suggested demand in Asset‑Light is inflecting higher, with daily revenue up about 24% year over year and shipments up roughly 17%, led by Managed Solutions. Revenue per shipment climbed around 7%, indicating that the segment is not only handling more freight but also capturing better yields as the quarter progresses.

Balance Sheet Strength Supports Capital Returns and Flexibility

Arcbest emphasized its strong financial position, noting ample liquidity and a net debt‑to‑EBITDA ratio well below the S&P 500 average. The company has returned more than $10 million to shareholders so far this year through buybacks and dividends, while preserving capacity for opportunistic share repurchases and high‑return growth investments.

Consolidated Profitability Under Pressure Despite Growth

Non‑GAAP consolidated operating income slid to $13 million from $17 million a year earlier, a decline of about 23.5% that underscores ongoing margin compression. Management acknowledged that while revenue is expanding, rising costs and mix shifts are limiting the ability to translate top‑line gains into bottom‑line improvement in the near term.

Earnings Per Share Compress as Costs Weigh on Results

Adjusted diluted earnings per share fell to $0.32 in the first quarter from $0.51 a year ago, a drop of roughly 37%. This EPS compression reflects the combination of softer margins, higher operating expenses, and only modest revenue growth, highlighting that the earnings recovery is lagging the early signs of demand stabilization.

Asset‑Based Operating Ratio Deterioration Highlights Margin Strain

The Asset‑Based business posted an operating ratio of 97.3%, 140 basis points worse than a year ago and 110 basis points weaker sequentially. This deterioration indicates that most of the profit pressure is concentrated in the asset‑heavy network, where higher expenses are outpacing pricing and productivity gains.

Higher Operating Costs Erode Margin Progress

Operating expenses were elevated as Arcbest added labor to support shipment growth and absorbed annual union wage increases along with higher fuel and depreciation costs. Investments in equipment and people are intended to support service and future growth, but in the short run they are compressing margins across the network.

Mix Shift Pressures Revenue per Hundredweight

Revenue per hundredweight in the Asset‑Based segment declined about 4% year over year, primarily due to a shift toward heavier freight that lowers yield metrics. Excluding fuel surcharge, April revenue per hundredweight fell in the low single digits, signaling that mix‑related pressure remains even as the company maintains discipline on base rates.

Weather and Uneven Demand Weigh on Short‑Term Volumes

Severe winter weather earlier in the quarter disrupted volumes and service, adding noise to demand trends. April saw shipments per day down about 1% year over year, though weight per shipment increased 6%, leaving daily tonnage up 5% and pointing to an uneven but gradually improving freight backdrop.

Fuel Volatility Complicates Near‑Term Margin Dynamics

Rising fuel prices boosted fuel surcharge revenue but also raised direct operating costs, creating timing mismatches between expense recognition and surcharge recovery. These swings in fuel cost and recovery are adding short‑term volatility to margins and contributing to the difficulty of converting revenue growth into profit.

Asset‑Based Profitability Declines Despite Volume Gains

Asset‑Based operating income declined by $9 million year over year, underscoring that this segment bore the brunt of the profitability erosion. Higher costs, mix shifts, and a weaker operating ratio more than offset the benefits of increased tonnage and disciplined pricing during the quarter.

Asset‑Light Guidance Flags Profitability Uncertainty

While Asset‑Light delivered $3 million of operating income in the first quarter, management guided to a second‑quarter range of about $1 million to $3 million. That outlook suggests that despite volume acceleration, evolving mix and scaling dynamics could limit near‑term margin expansion in the segment as it grows.

Forward‑Looking Guidance Balances Improvement and Risk

Looking ahead, management expects a stronger second quarter, led by a roughly 400 to 500 basis‑point sequential improvement in the Asset‑Based operating ratio and continued revenue and tonnage momentum. They are leaning on cost savings from continuous improvement and AI‑driven routing, while acknowledging that demand, freight mix, fuel, and Asset‑Light margin trends remain key variables for the pace of earnings recovery.

Arcbest’s earnings call painted a picture of a company executing well on productivity, technology, and cost control, yet still battling a tough margin environment. For investors, the story hinges on whether volume momentum and efficiency gains can outpace cost inflation and mix pressure, with the second quarter poised as an important test of the company’s cautiously optimistic outlook.

Investors heard a story of solid execution on technology and M&A priorities, offset by near‑term disruption in U.S. knees, salesforce changes and pockets of softness overseas.

Revenue Growth Shows Steady but Modest Momentum

Zimmer Biomet reported net sales of $2.087 billion, up 9.3% reported but just 2.9% on an organic constant currency basis excluding Paragon 28. U.S. sales rose 3.2% and international markets grew 2.5% in Q1 2026, signaling steady but not explosive demand as the company navigates product transitions and go‑to‑market changes.

Earnings Beat with Strong Adjusted and GAAP EPS

Adjusted EPS climbed to $2.09, roughly 15% higher than the prior year’s $1.81 on an adjusted basis, comfortably topping expectations. GAAP diluted EPS also improved to $1.22 versus $0.91 in the year‑ago quarter, underscoring improved profitability even after accounting for restructuring and acquisition‑related items.

Margins Hold Up as Full‑Year EPS Guidance Rises

The company posted an adjusted gross margin of 73% and an adjusted operating margin of 27.3%, both healthy levels in a competitive medtech landscape. Management boosted full‑year adjusted EPS guidance to a range of $8.40 to $8.55 from $8.30 to $8.45, signaling confidence that margin discipline can offset pricing pressure and higher interest expense.

Free Cash Flow Strengthens Balance Sheet Flexibility

Operating cash flow reached $359 million in Q1, translating into $246 million of free cash flow and leaving Zimmer Biomet with about $424 million in cash and equivalents. The company raised its 2026 free cash flow growth outlook to 9% to 11% from 8% to 10%, reinforcing its ability to fund investment, service debt and return capital to shareholders.

Technology and Robotics Power a High‑Growth Engine

Technology, data, bone cement and surgical solutions grew nearly 12% in the quarter, making them key growth drivers within the portfolio. Robotics and enabling technologies showed particular strength, with TMINI referenced as growing around 30%, continued ROSA momentum and OrthoGrid delivering its strongest quarter to date as surgeons adopt more digitally enabled tools.

Monogram Study Completion Advances Robotic Roadmap

Zimmer Biomet completed enrollment in its 102‑patient Monogram clinical study, marking an important milestone in its next‑generation robotic strategy. Management is targeting a semiautonomous Monogram launch in early 2027 and a fully autonomous version in late 2027 or early 2028, alongside plans to hire more than 200 robotic clinical sales reps by 2027 to support adoption.

Paragon 28 Integration Accelerates Top‑Line Contribution

The Paragon 28 acquisition added roughly 100 basis points to reported sales growth in 2026, underscoring its strategic value in foot and ankle. Growth in the business accelerated by about 200 basis points sequentially from Q4 2025 and is trending back toward double‑digit levels, suggesting that integration is progressing and synergies are beginning to show.

Capital Returns Supported by Share Repurchases

Zimmer Biomet repurchased $250 million of shares in the first quarter, ending with 195.8 million fully diluted shares outstanding. Management continues to assume up to $750 million of share repurchases for the full year, reflecting confidence in cash generation and a commitment to shareholder returns even amid elevated investment and debt service.

Operational Efficiency and Strategic Talent Additions

Management highlighted ongoing SKU rationalization, reduced days of inventory and expanded manufacturing in lower‑cost geographies as levers to support margins. The company also appointed Dr. Jonathan as a senior leader overseeing science, technology and medical affairs, with a mandate to shape its AI, robotics and software strategy and further differentiate the portfolio.

U.S. Knees Face Legacy Product and Market Headwinds

U.S. knee sales grew only 2.2% in Q1 as the firm phases out legacy implants such as NextGen and Vanguard, creating friction in near‑term growth. The business also lost two large accounts and saw added pressure from a West Coast Kaiser strike, highlighting that product transitions and external events are weighing on an otherwise solid reconstructive franchise.

Salesforce Transformation Brings Short‑Term Disruption

The shift to a more specialized, dedicated U.S. sales channel delivered productivity gains in territories that have already transitioned but caused modest disruption overall in the quarter. Management expects the transformation, which runs through the end of 2027, to require higher commercial investment and to create intermittent short‑term pressure as teams and territories are reset.

International Markets Trail U.S. Amid Channel Changes

International revenue lagged, growing 2.5% compared with the U.S., as distributor and go‑to‑market changes weighed on emerging markets such as China, the Middle East and parts of Europe. Some of the softness also reflected one‑time order timing issues, leaving room for improvement as new distribution structures stabilize and underlying procedure volumes recover.

Tariff Benefit Adds One‑Time Boost to Results

First‑quarter results were helped by a $0.20 benefit tied to invalidated tariffs and related refunds, which management described as partly pulling forward upside that had been expected for the second half. With roughly half of that benefit previously assumed later in the year, investors should view this tailwind as less durable and adjust expectations for run‑rate earnings power.

Pricing Pressure Remains a Structural Headwind

Consolidated pricing in the quarter was negative by about 40 basis points, reflecting ongoing competition and payer pressure across orthopedics. The company continues to assume up to 100 basis points of price erosion for full‑year 2026, underscoring the need for mix upgrades, cost controls and innovation to sustain margins in a deflationary pricing environment.

Higher Interest Costs Weigh on Nonoperating Line

Adjusted net interest and nonoperating expenses were about $71 million higher than a year ago, driven largely by debt taken on to fund the Paragon 28 acquisition. While the deal is helping top‑line growth, the additional leverage increases sensitivity to interest rates and raises the bar for returns on invested capital from future M&A.

Pockets of Weakness in Selected Product Segments

The SCP segment grew only 1.6%, with management citing continued challenges in restorative therapies and trauma businesses as key soft spots. These weaker areas highlight that not all parts of the portfolio are firing, and they could dilute overall growth until new products or restructuring efforts start to improve performance.

CFO Transition Adds Governance Overhang

CFO Suketu Upadhyay announced his departure, with Paul Stellato stepping in as interim CFO during a critical phase of commercial transformation. While there is no sign of strategic change, investors typically view senior finance transitions as a source of near‑term uncertainty, especially as Zimmer Biomet manages leverage, capital allocation and large technology bets.

Guidance Signals Confidence Despite Ongoing Headwinds

Looking ahead, Zimmer Biomet maintained its 2026 organic constant currency revenue growth outlook of 1% to 3%, implying reported growth of 2.5% to 4.5% with Paragon 28 adding about 100 basis points and a modest foreign exchange tailwind. The company lifted adjusted EPS guidance to $8.40 to $8.55 and free cash flow growth to 9% to 11%, while forecasting a slight year‑over‑year decline in operating and gross margins as it digests investments and pricing pressure.

Zimmer Biomet’s earnings call painted a picture of a medtech leader leaning into robotics, data and targeted M&A to drive long‑term growth, even as near‑term execution is complicated by channel changes and product sunsets. For investors, the raised EPS and cash flow guidance, solid tech momentum and disciplined capital returns are clear positives, tempered by sales disruption, higher interest costs and management transition risk.

Improved Profitability and EPS Growth

Avidbank delivered a sharp step‑up in earnings, with Q1 2026 net income rising to $9.0 million, or $0.84 per diluted share, versus $6.9 million and $0.65 in Q4. Returns also moved higher, as return on assets improved to 1.46% from 1.12% and return on average equity increased to 12.7%, signaling more efficient use of the balance sheet.

Strong Loan and Deposit Growth

Balance‑sheet momentum remained solid, with loans up $24 million in the quarter and $332 million, or about 18%, since March 31, 2025. Deposits increased $13 million in Q1 and are up roughly $270 million, or 14%, over the same period, and management reiterated expectations for low double‑digit loan and deposit growth for the full year.

Net Interest Margin Expansion

Net interest margin expanded to 4.38% in Q1, up 25 basis points from Q4 and reflecting both core spread strength and some one‑time tailwinds. The quarter benefited from a special FHLB dividend worth roughly four basis points and the absence of a prior‑quarter interest reversal that had reduced Q4 margin by about 12 basis points.

Lower Credit Provision and Better Coverage

Provision for credit losses fell to $1.4 million from $2.8 million in Q4, effectively halving the quarterly build. Nonperforming loans declined by $16.3 million, aided by a construction loan payoff and charge‑offs, which together point to reduced measured problem assets even as management continues to emphasize vigilant credit oversight.

Efficiency and Capital Metrics Improved

Operating efficiency moved in the right direction, with the efficiency ratio improving to 50.4%, underscoring better cost control relative to revenue. Capital strength also increased, as book value per share rose to $26.33, Tier 1 capital climbed to 11.39%, and the bank repurchased 25,000 shares for about $693,000 at an average price of $27.69.

Core Fee Income Strength

Beyond interest income, the bank benefited from higher core banking fee streams such as service charges, foreign exchange, and credit card revenues. These recurring fees helped offset weakness in more volatile noninterest income categories, giving Avidbank a sturdier earnings base as markets remain choppy.

Balanced Talent Investment for Growth

Headcount increased modestly to 154 as Avidbank added three employees in Q1 and signaled plans to hire more bankers over coming quarters. Management framed these additions as deliberate investments to scale the franchise after a successful IPO, aiming to extend relationship depth without undermining cost discipline.

Credit Charges and Net Charge-Offs

Credit costs were not entirely benign, as net charge‑offs reached $2.8 million in Q1, equal to 52 basis points of average loans. The impact was concentrated in two commercial and industrial credits, which management described as a meaningful but manageable near‑term credit hit within the broader portfolio.

Venture and SaaS Exposure Under Scrutiny

Roughly $165 million of exposure is tied to venture lending, including SaaS borrowers, an area executives highlighted as requiring active monitoring. Horizontal SaaS was called out as riskier than vertical SaaS, with about $4 million of loans in the horizontal segment criticized or classified and more than 60 early‑stage accounts being watched for fundraising and business‑model risk.

Deposit Cost Pressure and Margin Headwinds

Funding costs remain a key swing factor, with the average cost of interest‑bearing deposits at 2.98% for Q1 and spot costs at 3.03% by March 31. Management expects deposit costs to stay above 3% and sees some near‑term upward pressure, leading them to project NIM moderation toward roughly 4.25% to 4.30% from the current 4.38%.

Noninterest Income Decline

Total noninterest income slipped to $1.5 million from $1.8 million in the prior quarter, a decline of about 16.7%. The drop was driven mainly by lower warrant and success fee income, along with reduced returns from fund investments, reminding investors that some revenue sources remain inherently volatile.

Rising Operating Expenses Expected

Noninterest expense edged up to $14.1 million, an increase of about $231,000 or 1.7% quarter over quarter, and management signaled more pressure ahead. Salaries and benefits, currently around $9.6 million, are expected to move toward roughly $10 million as hiring continues, pushing expenses higher even as the bank seeks to maintain its improved efficiency ratio.

Elevated DDA Levels and Timing Variability

Non‑interest‑bearing demand deposits represented 33.1% of total deposits in Q1, boosted by late‑quarter inflows from clients. Executives cautioned that this mix is likely elevated and should normalize toward a lower level, introducing timing‑related volatility in both liquidity management and margin performance.

Asset Downgrade and Tenant Risk

The criticized loan bucket increased due to a downgrade of a real estate relationship tied to two South Bay office buildings facing a near‑term tenant vacancy. While the credits are currently performing and carry low loan‑to‑value ratios, management chose to downgrade and monitor them closely, underscoring a conservative stance on emerging commercial real estate risks.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, management reaffirmed expectations for low double‑digit loan and deposit growth, while guiding NIM to ease toward the 4.25%–4.30% range as deposit costs remain above 3%. They project DDA balances to normalize toward the mid‑20% range, maintain a mid‑28% tax rate, anticipate modest expense drift from ongoing hiring, and continue active capital deployment and tight oversight of venture and SaaS credit exposures.

Avidbank’s earnings call painted a picture of a franchise gaining earnings power and scale, supported by robust growth, improving efficiency, and solid capital. The upside is tempered by clearly defined risks around venture lending, select real estate exposures, and funding costs, leaving investors with a measured but constructive view of the bank’s trajectory through 2026.
